text: The surgeon locates the anatomical plane between the prostate and the adjacent neurovascular bundles, preparing for careful dissection.
We'll try to keep the nerves in the patient. These nerves are believed to play a role in continence as well as erections. So that's why we try to keep the nerves behind.

The surgeon carefully peels the nerves from the surrounding tissue layer, ensuring the dissection remains well away from the nerves. Blood vessels are cauterized as they are encountered during the dissection.
The surgeon begins using clips to secure structures, avoiding heat or thermal injury to the nearby nerves, which are visible in the surgical field.
All right, you have your clipper?
The surgeon applies a clip to secure a structure, ensuring no thermal injury to the nearby nerves within the neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon applies clips to the blood vessels, avoiding thermal injury to nearby nerves, which are preserved for their role in continence and erections.
The surgeon locates the nerve within the tissue layer, ensuring it remains undisturbed while instructing the assistant to apply a clip nearby.
Good. And one more. Yep. Good. Good.
